titleStmt.title: Response to David Steigmann’s discussion of our paper

affiliation.orgName: Institute of Engineering Mechanics & Structural Analysis
Faculty of Civil and Environmental Engineering
Aachen Institute for Advanced Study in Computational Engineering Science (AICES)

analytic.title: Response to David Steigmann’s discussion of our paper

biblStruct.note: Submitted on 16 October 2023; Accepted on 29 November 2023

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We thank Prof. David Steigmann for his discussion (Steigmann, 2024), in particular his proof on the general validity of his internal power expression (see Eq. ( 8) below) and his expression for the variation of the geodesic curvature. His proof allows us to clarify two misleading statements in Duong et al. (2023), and confirm that its formulation is fully consistent with the formulation of Steigmann ( 2018). However, some of our original statements made in Remark 4.9 are not wrong: The third term in (8) can lead to spurious constitutive models for in-plane bending, which was our original concern.
The discussion below follows the notation in Duong et al. (2023).

We agree with Steigmann that our statement in Remark 4.8 is incorrect. This error stems from our incomplete proof provided in Appendix 5 of Duong et al. (2023), which is based on the incorrect assumption that the variation of the fiber director, δc α , is not solely expressible through the variation of the metric, δa αβ . Instead,
div.formula: δc γ = 1 2 c γ c αβ δa αβ ,(1)
div.p: i.e. δc α is solely expressible through δa αβ . Eq. ( 1) follows from the variations δJ = J a αβ δa αβ /2, δλ = L αβ δa αβ /(2λ), the identity a αβ = ℓ αβ + c αβ that follows from Eq. ( 8) in Duong et al. (2023), and the relation
div.formula: c γ = J λ -1 c 0 γ ,(2)
div.p: given in Steigmann's discussion. Here, c α := c • a α , J = det s F , λ = ∥F L∥, c 0 α := c 0 • A α , c αβ := c α c β , c α := c • a α , ℓ αβ := ℓ α ℓ β , ℓ α := ℓ • a α , L αβ := L α L β and L α := L • A α . We thank Steigmann for providing relation (2). We were not aware of this relation in our derivation of Appendix 5 (Duong et al., 2023), since we had used
div.formula: δc α = -ℓ β α c • δa β + c • δa α(3)
div.p: (see Eq. ( 213) of Duong et al. (2023)) instead of Eq. (1). We note here that Eq. ( 3) is not wrong. Instead, it is equivalent to Eq. ( 1). This can be shown by applying the identities c • δa α = δ β α c • δa β and δ β α = ℓ β α + c β α (following from Eq. ( 8) in Duong et al. (2023)) to (3) and using the symmetry of c αβ . Relation (2) can also be used to show that the geodesic curvature given in Eq. ( 6) of Steigmann's discussion,
div.formula: κ g = J λ -3 L αβ c 0 γ S γ αβ -J λ -3 L αβ c 0 α;β ,(4)
div.p: is identical to Eq. ( 51) in Duong et al. (2023), i.e.
div.formula: κ g = ℓ αβ c γ S γ αβ + λ -1 c α ℓ β L α ;β ,(5)
div.p: This follows from Eq. ( 2), L α = λ ℓ α and L α c 0 α;β = -c 0 α L α ;β (cf. Eq. ( 38) in Duong et al. (2023)). With variation (1), we can now continue the derivation in Appendix 5 of Duong et al. (2023): Starting from Eq. ( 5) and using Eq. ( 1) together with Eqs. ( 208) and ( 210) from Duong et al. (2023), we arrive at the expression
div.formula: κg = ℓ αβ c γ Ṡγ αβ + 1 2 κ g (a αβ -3 ℓ αβ ) ȧαβ ,(6)
div.p: which is also found in Steigmann's discussion. That is, the rate of geodesic curvature, κg , is fully expressible in terms of ȧαβ and Ṡγ αβ . Since these are symmetric w.r.t. α and β, the stress symmetrization employed in the power balance of Steigmann (2018) is indeed general, contrary to what was written in Remark 4.8. We thank Prof. Steigmann for pointing out this error.
Eq. ( 6) allows us to confirm that our internal power expression given in Eq. ( 107) of Duong et al. (2023),
div.formula: P int = 1 2 R 0 τ αβ ȧαβ dA + R 0 M αβ 0 ḃαβ dA + R 0 μ0 κg dA ,(7)
div.p: is equivalent to the internal power expression given in Steigmann (2018), cf. Eq. ( 63), which reads
div.formula: P int = 1 2 R 0 τ αβ ȧαβ dA + R 0 M αβ 0 ḃαβ dA + R 0 M αβ 0γ Ṡγ αβ dA ,(8)
div.p: in our notation. This follows from inserting Eq. ( 6) into ( 7) and redefining the stress as
div.formula: τ αβ ← τ αβ + μ0 κ g (a αβ -3 ℓ αβ ) .(9)
div.p: Eq. ( 7) is also equivalent to
div.formula: P int = 1 2 R 0 τ αβ ȧαβ dA + R 0 M αβ 0 ḃαβ dA + R 0 M αβ 0 ḃαβ dA ,(10)
div.p: the internal power expression proposed in Duong et al. (2023) (see Eq. ( 113) there). Therefore the internal power expressions given by Steigmann (2018) (i.e. Eq. ( 8)) and our expression in Eq. ( 10) are equivalent. However, the last term in (8) can lead to spurious constitutive models for in-plane bending, as is shown below.
Concerning Steigmann's discussion of Remark 4.9
Steigmann's discussion of Remark 4.9 concerns two points: The well-definedness of the in-plane bending power term, and its parametrization-dependence.

We agree that the internal power of Steigmann, see (8), is not wrong and does not miss any contributions in the present context. This was shown above. Also it does not depend on the surface parameterization (i.e. the choice of curvilinear coordinate system), as Steigmann rightly points out. However, there is still an issue with the last term in (8): It can lead to constitutive models for in-plane bending that are not well-defined for some deformations.
Steigmann's expression (8) leads to the constitutive equations for the membrane and bending stresses,
div.formula: τ αβ = 2 ∂W ∂a αβ , M αβ 0 = ∂W ∂b αβ , M αβ 0γ = ∂W ∂S γ αβ ,(11)
div.p: based on the stored energy function W = W a αβ , b αβ , S γ αβ . Our expression (10) on the other hand, leads to the constitutive equations
div.formula: τ αβ = 2 ∂W ∂a αβ , M αβ 0 = ∂W ∂b αβ , M αβ 0 = ∂W ∂ bαβ , (12
div.p: based on the stored energy function W = Ŵ a αβ , b αβ , bαβ .

In general, the in-plane bending behavior of fibers should be related to a change of their curvature κ g . Thus, the in-plane bending energy should be a function of κ g . This means that in general the in-plane bending energy cannot be a function of S γ αβ alone, as it does not fully describe κ g according to (4). The problem with the third terms in Eqs. ( 8) and ( 11) is that they suggest precisely that. Indeed, such a function has been proposed in Steigmann and Dell'Isola (2015), cf. Eq. ( 60), and used in the computational model of Schulte et al. (2020), cf. Eq. ( 42). It can lead to spurious bending moments as is shown in the example below. In our formulation this problem does not appear, since κ g is fully described by bαβ , i.e. κ g = bαβ ℓ αβ , cf. Eq. ( 50) of Duong et al. (2023).
The issue can be illustrated by the following example, see Fig. 1. We consider a ring-shaped domain described by the parameterization
div.formula: X(r, ϕ) = r cos ϕ e 1 + r sin ϕ e 2 , 0 ≤ ϕ < 2π , r 1 ≤ r ≤ r 2 , (13
div.p: with a circular fiber located at r = r 0 . The domain is deformed by applying the displacement field

where a 1 and a 2 are parameters of u that have sufficiently small magnitude to avoid singularities in the deformation x = X + u. For any admissible a i , displacement u is zero along the fiber, and hence does not change its curvature, which remains equal to 1/r 0 . (The fiber is only stretched in lateral direction.) Without curvature change, no fiber bending moment should appear. However, according to the model of Steigmann and Dell'Isola (2015), the third term in (11) generates the fiber bending moment
div.formula: (see Appendix A) m0 ∼ a 1 cos ϕ + a 2 sin ϕ ,(15)
div.p: which increases with a i , see Fig. 2015) is a natural choice following from Eq. ( 8). Therefore Eq. ( 8), which itself is correct, can suggest spurious bending models.

As the example shows, the last term in Eq. ( 11) can generate a fiber moment that depends inconsistently on the deformation, seen here through the surface deformation parameters a i . However, the third term in Eq. ( 8) does not depend on the surface parametrization, as was written imprecisely in Remark 4.9. 13) and ( 14): a. Initial configuration for r 1 = 1, r 0 = 2 and r 2 = 3; b. Deformed configuration for a 1 = 0.25 and a 2 = 0; c. Corresponding fiber bending moment m0 according to the constitutive models of Steigmann and Dell'Isola (2015) and Duong et al. (2023). The former predicts an increase of m0 with the deformation, even though the fiber curvature does not change. Here a := a 1 cos ϕ + a 2 sin ϕ.

div.head: Concerning the remaining points raised by Steigmann
div.p: Steigmann claims that our "force and torque balance laws are postulated on the basis of free-body diagrams without reference to the Principle of Virtual Power". This is not true. Our formulation constructs the force and torque balance laws and the constitutive equations systematically from linear momentum balance, angular momentum balance, and the mechanical power balance in Secs. 3.3, 3.4 and Sec 3.5 of Duong et al. (2023). The latter balance is mathematically equivalent to the principle of virtual power.

Steigmann further states that our formulation does "not make explicit the famous Kirchhoff boundary conditions and corner forces established by Kirchhoff". Such corner forces are fully contained in our formulation. They appear when the boundary traction T is replaced by its effective counterpart and the domain boundary of the shell surface is not smooth, which is shown in Appendix B below.
The corner forces are part of the external virtual work, and so the internal virtual work expression presented in Duong et al. (2023) is unaffected.
Singular boundary fibers are indeed not studied in our work. But they do not affect the examples presented in Duong et al. (2023) and Duong et al. (2022).

Appendix A: Derivation of the bending moments in the example of Fig. 1 Introducing c := cos ϕ and s := sin ϕ, the deformation defined by Eqs. ( 13) and ( 14) has the tangent vectors along parameters r and ϕ,
and their derivatives
From this follows
div.formula: ) det[a αβ ] = g 2 r 2 , g := 1 + (a 1 c + a 2 s) r(19)
div.p: and
div.formula: a αβ = 1 g 2 r 2 r 2 (a 1 s -a 2 c) r 2 (a 1 s -a 2 c) r 2 (c + a 1 r) 2 + (s + a 2 r) 2 , (20
div.p: which leads to the dual tangent vectors
div.formula: a 1 = c e 1 + s e 2 g , a 2 = -(s + a 2 r) e 1 + (c + a 1 r) e 2 g r . (21
div.p: The Christoffel symbols thus become
div.formula: Γ 1 αβ = 1 g a 1 c + a 2 s 0 0 -r , Γ 2 αβ = 1 g r a 2 c -a 1 s g g (a 2 c -a 1 s) r 2 . (22
div.p: The corresponding quantities for the reference configuration (A α , A α,β , A αβ , A αβ , A α and Γγ αβ ) follow from this with a 1 = a 2 = 0 and g = 1. Thus
div.formula: S 1 αβ := Γ 1 αβ -Γ1 αβ = a 1 c + a 2 s g 1 0 0 r 2 , S 2 αβ := Γ 2 αβ -Γ2 αβ = a 2 c -a 1 s g r 1 0 0 r 2 ,(23)
div.p: which is only zero for vanishing a i . Further J = det[a αβ ]/ det[A αβ ] = g.
For the fiber at r = r 0 , the fiber direction (before and after deformation) is
such that
With this, the curvature-invariant of Steigmann and Dell'Isola (2015) follows as
div.formula: g L := L α L β S γ αβ a γ = a 1 e 1 + a 2 e 2 ,(26)
div.p: such that fiber bending energy of Steigmann and Dell'Isola (2015),
div.formula: W SD := B 2 g L • g L = B 2 a 2 1 + a 2 2 ,(27)
div.p: increases with a 1 and a 2 . Here, B is the material constant for in-plane bending.
The fiber director follows from ℓ by a counterclockwise rotation of 90
With this and L ,2 = -c e 1 -s e 2 , the two contributions of the geodesic curvature in (4) become
div.formula: κ Γ g := ℓ α ℓ β S γ αβ c γ = -a 1 c -a 2 s ,(30) and κ
L g := λ -1 c α ℓ β A α • L ,β = c 1 ℓ 2 A 1 • L ,2 = 1 r 0 + a 1 c + a 2 s ,(31)
div.p: for the present case, where there is no fiber stretch (λ = 1). The full geodesic curvature thus is
div.formula: κ g = κ Γ g + κ L g = 1 r 0 ,(32)
div.p: which is the expected value for the example. This is equal to the geodesic curvature in the reference configuration (i.e. κ 0 g = 1/r 0 ). All the relative curvature measures of Duong et al. (2023) are thus zero, and the in-plane bending energy of Duong et al. (2023),
vanishes for all a i . In our formulation the in-plane bending moment in the fiber is
which gives m0 = B κ g -κ 0 g = 0, here. Eq. ( 34
for general fibers with L α = λ ℓ α . With Eqs. ( 23), ( 25) and (29) of the present example, this becomes mSD 0 = B (a 1 c + a 2 s), leading directly to Eq. ( 15).

figure.figDesc: 1c. On the other hand, for the constitutive model proposed inDuong et al. (2023), no fiber bending moment appears. The reason for the spurious moment in Eq. (15) lies in the constitutive model ofSteigmann and Dell'Isola (2015) that is based on S γ αβ alone. As seen in Eq. (4) -and illustrated by the example in Sec. 7.1 of Duong et al. (2023) -S γ αβ yields an incomplete curvature description. The constitutive model of Steigmann and Dell'Isola (

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: Deformation example of Eqs. (13) and (14): a. Initial configuration for r 1 = 1, r 0 = 2 and r 2 = 3; b. Deformed configuration for a 1 = 0.25 and a 2 = 0; c. Corresponding fiber bending moment m0 according to the constitutive models ofSteigmann and Dell'Isola (2015) andDuong et al. (2023). The former predicts an increase of m0 with the deformation, even though the fiber curvature does not change. Here a := a 1 cos ϕ + a 2 sin ϕ.

) follows from Eqs. (82b), (108c) and (110c) ofDuong et al. (2023) with ν = ℓ and m0 = J m. The corresponding (equivalent) bending moment according to formulation (8) is m0 = M αβ 0 γ ℓ α ℓ β c γ . (35)This follows from inserting (6) and μ0 = m0 into (7) and comparing corresponding terms with (8).Here ℓ α ℓ α = 1 and c α c α = 1 have been used. For the constitutive model ofSteigmann and Dell'Isola (2015) followsM αβ 0 γ = B L αβ L µη S δ µη a γδ(36)from Eqs. (11c) and (27). Eq. (35) then yields mSD 0 = B λ 2 S γ αβ L α L β c γ ,

div.head: Appendix B: Corner forces in our formulation
div.p: Corner forces appear when the external virtual work expression (here for a single fiber family)
Eq. ( 135.3) in Duong et al. (2023)) is rewritten following the procedure used in our previous work (Sauer and Duong (2017), cf. Sec 6.3). This starts with the balance of angular momentum

(cf. Eq. ( 87) in Duong et al. (2023)), where m := m τ τ + m ν ν + m n is the complete boundary bending moment defined in Eq. ( 66) in Duong et al. (2023). Using ν = τ × n, n = -τ × ν and τ := ∂x/∂s = x ′ , the last two terms in Eq. ( 39) can be rewritten as
The last expression shows that the moment components m ν and m contribute to the effective boundary traction
With this, δx ′ • n = -τ • δn and δx ′ = τ α δa α (following from τ • n = 0 and x ′ = τ α a α with τ α := ∂ξ α /∂s being fixed), we can write
Inserting Eq. ( 42) into Eq. ( 38) and using M = m τ ν -m ν τ (cf. Eq. ( 58

where the last two terms are jump terms that appear at corners on ∂S due to the out-of-plane boundary moment m ν and the in-plane boundary moment m. Effectively, these moments act as point forces at those corners. Contrary to m ν , m also contributes to another term in G ext -the fourth term in Eq. ( 43). This is also seen in the formulation of Steigmann (2018), cf. Eq. ( 87).

analytic.title: A general isogeometric finite element formulation for rotation-free shells with in-plane bending of embedded fibers
A general theory for anisotropic Kirchhoff-Love shells with in-plane bending of embedded fibers
On the theoretical foundations of solid and liquid shells
Isogeometric analysis of fiber reinforced composites using Kirchhoff-Love shell elements
Discussion of "A general theory for anisotropic Kirchhoff-Love shells with in-plane bending of embedded fibers
Mechanical response of fabric sheets to three-dimensional bending, twisting, and stretching
